coredata.dc:title: Luminescence-based techniques to monitor pre-, post-harvest and processing changes in fresh produce and derivatives: Principles, applications and trends
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, M.
Wang, K.
Chen, C.
Corradini, M.G.
coredata.dc:description:
Background Maintaining and ensuring food safety and quality of fresh or minimally processed produce is a priority for producers, distributors, suppliers and consumers. The transient nature and dynamism of the fresh produce supply chain and the high perishability of the products require rapid, cost-effective and noninvasive methods for monitoring food integrity (quality, safety and identity) along the supply chain.
Scope and approach This review covers luminescence-based methods that target intrinsic or extrinsic fluorophores as reporters of food safety, freshness, and authenticity and the requirements for assessment of these attributes in produce at pre- and post-harvest stages.
Key findings Steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, excitation-emission matrices (EEMs), and hyperspectral imaging in fluorescence mode (HSI) offer in-line capabilities due to advances in data acquisition and processing. This includes relevant feature extraction, classification, and modeling using machine learning and mechanistic approaches. Advances in data integration, wireless networks and cloud-based computing can enhance the applicability of these methods to monitor and inform continuous and effective decision-making along the supply chain and assist in providing feedback to virtual objects/systems such as digital twins. The use of spatially resolved techniques other than HSI, such as Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) or super-resolution microscopy (SRM), although underexplored in foods, offers a powerful option to explore mechanisms that affect food integrity in fresh produce at pre- and post-harvest stages.
Significance and conclusions Luminescence-based methods can significantly improve supply chain transparency and inform advanced supply chain modeling by providing quick, low-cost, in-line capabilities to identify and mitigate future safety and quality breaches.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Among the supervised algorithms, K-nearest neighbors (KNN), support vector machines (SVM) and random forest (RF) are the most frequently reported for the analysis of spectral data in produce. KNN divides the data into pre-set clusters based on the distance between the values of different features (Zhu et al., 2021). The closer the values that a sample's attribute/s exhibit in relation to a reference point, the greater the likelihood that that sample belongs to a particular category or class (Zhu et al., 2021). Ebersbach et al. (2018) used KNN for the analysis of HSI data to classify cannabis varieties into high and low producers of bioactive compounds, as this data analysis technique better represented the nonlinear nature of the data rather than using a linear unmixing model. This analysis allowed differentiating cell types based on secondary metabolite content while also identifying growth conditions leading to better performance. SVMs are primarily used for classification but can also perform regression tasks, in which case they are dubbed support vector regression (SVR) (Gandhi, 2018). The goal of SVM is to find a hyperplane that allows for classification in the N-dimensional space (where N represents the number of features) to uniquely represent the sample data points (Gandhi, 2018). SVM is effective even if the number of dimensions is similar to the sample number, efficient in memory and versatile; however, overfitting may occur (Scikit, 2024). Zheng et al. (2010) used SVM to classify jujube fruits based on post-harvest quality based on chlorophyll fluorescence emission intensity during photosynthesis and minimum and maximum respiration rates collected using fiber optics, achieving 87\u201393 % correct classification depending on the type of classifier used, e.g., RBF (radical basis function), sigmoidal or polynomial SVMs. The study identified correlations between photophysical parameters that allowed gaining insights into the degree of ripeness of jujube in real-time. More recently, Bertani et al. (2020) also used SVM to analyze fluorescence spectra of almond slurry to detect potential aflatoxin contamination, achieving 95 % classification accuracy.The authors gratefully acknowledge support from the NSERC Discovery program and CFI-JELF. Ms. Maleeka Singh would also like to express her gratitude to the Arrell Food Institute, the SMART Training Program, Food from Thought, and the Ontario Agri-Food Innovation Alliance for their support to conduct her graduate studies at the University of Guelph.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Data analysis strategies commonly applied to luminescence spectroscopy data.
Example of differences in EEMs of a fresh vegetable product after being adulterated, stored and thermally processed.

Processes and elements potentially involved in the development of a digital twin of a vegetable product, including its pre- and post-harvest food status. The data acquisition (left-bottom) comprising luminescence-based fingerprinting methods is collected and analyzed through smart and cloud computing (middle and bottom right). The IoT or cloud computing platform provides a framework for the connection between luminescence-based methods, smart technology, and data analysis tools (such as AI and machine learning) that allows for the link between the actual product and its digital model (avatar). This will then allow for the assessment of a food's status, i.e., whether there is a risk to its safety or quality or whether it meets safety or quality standards (top-right).
Intrinsic lumiphores frequently found in food (including produce) commodities.

Effective reduction of data dimensionality and variability, elimination of multicollinearity
Helpful in visualizing complex data.
Data are converted into new PC domains and may lose their original physical meaning; may not capture nonlinear correlations.
Clustering fruits and vegetables based on quality attributes/safety risk by analyzing luminescence peaks/bands.
Ahmad et al. (2017); Sarimov et al. (2021); Delwiche et al. (2019)
Decomposes multi-way data into single factors; able to handle multiple datasets.
Sensitive to scattering (Raman and Rayleigh) and noise; difficult to interpret higher-order components.
Suitable for multi-dimensional fluorescence data analysis, e.g., EEMs for identity, quality and safety classification.
Acković et al. (2018); Sádecká and Tóthová (2007)
Easy visualization of groups with similar attributes via dendrograms without the need for predefined labels.
Sensitive to noise and outliers; does not provide quantitative performance metrics for model quality evaluation.
Classification of commercial olive oils from EEMs data andquality attributes.
Reduction in model overfitting by selecting subsets of informative variables; elimination of multicollinearity; maintenance of original data domains.
Sensitive to outliers; may get trapped in local minima when dealing with high-dimensional data; may require manual intervention in complex cases.
Effective feature (bands) selection from HSI datasets for assessing quality decay in fruits and vegetables.
Effective separation of labelled data groups; robust even with high-dimensional datasets.
Assumes data normality and linearity, which may not be accurate for complex datasets.
Authentication of fruit and vegetable products during postharvest quality control.
Effective in dealing with nonlinear data; no explicit training required.
Dependent on the choice of the K value; sensitive to noise.
Classification of freshness of fruits and vegetable oils; detection of bacteria biofilms.
Dankowska and Kowalewski (2019); Lee et al. (2021)
Effective for nonlinear classification via kernel tricks with high-dimensional data; useful for qualitative and quantitative analysis.
Requires careful selection of kernels and parameters.
Detection of aflatoxins in dried tree nuts; classification of fruit postharvest quality and damages.
Fu and Wang (2022); Bertani et al. (2020)
Suitable for large and complex data even with missing values; useful for qualitative and quantitative analysis.
Susceptible to overfitting; difficulty in interpreting the results due to its ensemble nature.
Classification and identification of adulterants in produce.
Jiang et al. (2023); de Santana et al. (2019)
Simple to understand and implement; suitable for linear correlations.
Assumes data linearity; multicollinearity in high-dimensional data; prone to overfitting when applied to complex problems.
Quantification and identification of safety risks (model infections) in grains.
Ability to reduce multicollinearity; works efficiently with smaller datasets.
Requires extensive preprocessing to handle outliers.
Prediction of fruit maturity or identification of cultivars.
Armstrong et al. (2023); Birenboim et al. (2023)
Combines PCA with regression for quantitative analysis.
Limited predictive power for non-linear relationships.
Moisture, total soluble solids content and water activity assessment from luminescence data.
Flexible model architecture; ability to handle large numbers of features and complex data.
Requires a large number of accurately labelled data; black-box model with limited physical or chemical interpretation.
Fruit safety risks, quality decay, fraud prediction and detection.
Li et al. (2022); Ariana et al. (2006)
Particularly robust for imaging data; captures spatial hierarchies in the data.
High computational power. black-box model without physical and chemical interpretation.
Prediction of fruit maturity based on multiple response variables.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Maintaining and ensuring food safety and quality of fresh or minimally processed produce is a priority for producers, distributors, suppliers and consumers. The transient nature and dynamism of the fresh produce supply chain and the high perishability of the products require rapid, cost-effective and noninvasive methods for monitoring food integrity (quality, safety and identity) along the supply chain.

This review covers luminescence-based methods that target intrinsic or extrinsic fluorophores as reporters of food safety, freshness, and authenticity and the requirements for assessment of these attributes in produce at pre- and post-harvest stages.

Steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, excitation-emission matrices (EEMs), and hyperspectral imaging in fluorescence mode (HSI) offer in-line capabilities due to advances in data acquisition and processing. This includes relevant feature extraction, classification, and modeling using machine learning and mechanistic approaches. Advances in data integration, wireless networks and cloud-based computing can enhance the applicability of these methods to monitor and inform continuous and effective decision-making along the supply chain and assist in providing feedback to virtual objects/systems such as digital twins. The use of spatially resolved techniques other than HSI, such as Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) or super-resolution microscopy (SRM), although underexplored in foods, offers a powerful option to explore mechanisms that affect food integrity in fresh produce at pre- and post-harvest stages.

Luminescence-based methods can significantly improve supply chain transparency and inform advanced supply chain modeling by providing quick, low-cost, in-line capabilities to identify and mitigate future safety and quality breaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Continuous, rather than inferential, monitoring of a food product's safety and quality parameters allows for improved control, transparency and evaluation of the food supply chain (Knott et al., 2023). In parallel, increased government and consumer concerns require a better and more extensive implementation of food management systems to improve and ensure the safety, quality, affordability and sustainability of food products. These trends are of particular importance for foods of vegetal origin, especially fresh produce, due to their high susceptibility to spoilage and increasing share in our diet.

Fresh produce consumption has risen in recent years due to its human and environmental benefits (GOC, 2024). In fact, Canada's Food Guide and the EAT-Lancet Commission recommend that plant-based and produce-rich diets become the norm (GOC, 2024; Willett et al., 2019). Despite the advantages of a produce-rich diet, the production of fruits and vegetables is limited, and current yields do not meet the requirements advanced by current health guidelines (KC et al., 2018). Also, fruit and vegetable perishability translates into short shelf lives, which are further shortened by spoilage microorganisms (bacteria, molds and yeasts) or foodborne pathogens (GOC, 2024), often indigenous or introduced through pre- and post-harvest processes. This accelerates deteriorative reactions, reducing the availability and quality of produce, increasing waste, and/or causing food safety risks to consumers.

Food fraud, or economically motivated adulteration, has also been reported to permeate the market in high-value food products such as organic produce, olive oil and juices, which further hampers food safety and quality (Singh, 2020). As the food supply chain is highly dynamic and needs to address the requirements of getting high-quality, safe and authentic foods for consumers, there is a need for the development of tools with quick, real-time, and cost-effective monitoring capabilities.

Spectroscopic tools, such as those based on luminescence, Raman or IR properties of food components, provide rapid, non-destructive, non-invasive and sensitive measures of food attributes before, during and post-processing (Colaruotolo et al., 2021). Luminescence-based fingerprinting methods have been utilized more frequently in the recent literature as a process analytical technology (PAT) for a variety of foods including, but not limited to, dairy (milk, yogurt, cheeses), fish, fruits and vegetables (Rahman et al., 2024; Lelis et al., 2022; Mori et al., 2024). This is due to the high sensitivity of the associated techniques, as well as the environmental sensitivity of intrinsic (i.e., naturally present) or extrinsic (i.e., purposely added) lumiphores, which allows reporting on properties beyond just merely concentration (Corradini et al., 2016). Honing non-destructive techniques such as luminescence-based fingerprinting methods can better inform early warning systems, ensuring food safety and quality. With improvements in portable spectroscopic devices and the ability to connect these systems through smart technologies and networks, in-line and rapid screening along the supply chain is possible. Additionally, the use of virtual objects or systems, also known as digital twins, has been gaining traction in simulating real systems or objects using complex, real-time data connected through smart technologies, allowing decision-makers to make more informed and better decisions (Corradini et al., 2024; Udugama et al., 2023; Verboven et al., 2020).

While luminescence-based fingerprinting methods have been applied to assess various food types as discussed above, this review focuses on the use of luminescence-based analytical techniques for evaluating pre- and post-harvest integrity (i.e., quality, safety and authenticity) of fresh produce and their products. It overviews the basic principles of luminescence-based analysis/sensing, extant and emerging techniques for capturing and analyzing data, and their applications in food integrity monitoring. It discusses the requirements for collecting spectral data and integrating the rich and complex signals into data analytics tools to support the optimal performance of the supply chain. Lastly, it examines how luminescence-based methods can be coupled with smart technologies and emerging concepts like digital twins to improve supply chain transparency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles of luminescence-based analysis and sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Lumiphores are compounds that emit a photon upon deexcitation after absorption of ultraviolet or visible light (Jameson, 2014). Photoluminescence can be mainly classified into fluorescence and phosphorescence. Upon photoexcitation, a lumiphore transitions from its ground state (S0) to a higher energy vibrational singlet (excited) or triplet (excited) state, and as the molecule returns to the S0, light is emitted (fluorescence or phosphorescence, respectively). The structure of the lumiphores, and in some cases their concentration, dictates their photophysical properties, i.e., quantum yield (QY), intensity, energy, polarity, and lifetime. Their photophysical properties can also be sensitive to the environment's characteristics. For example, the polarity of the solvent (particularly for polar fluorophores), its pH (for fluorophores with carboxy or hydroxy residues), and the medium viscosity (for luminescent molecular rotors) may affect luminescence intensity and lifetime (Jameson, 2014). It should be noted that among the photophysical properties of the lumiphores, luminescence emission intensity is concentration-dependent, while polarization and lifetimes, i.e., the time between reaching the excited state and its decay to the ground state (S0), are concentration-independent (Corradini et al., 2016). Polarization and anisotropy describe the effect of polarized light on the lumiphores, which leads to photoselective excitation. As such, fluorescence anisotropy can report on factors, such as binding, conformational changes and aggregation, that may affect the rotational diffusion rate of the lumiphore (Jameson, 2014). Other phenomena, besides the lumiphore's photophysical properties, can affect luminescence measurement or a lumiphore's performance as a sensor. The most notable is Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which occurs when the emission spectrum of a lumiphore overlaps with the excitation spectrum of another molecule so that the energy is transferred from the former molecule (donor) to the latter (acceptor). FRET is highly dependent on the distance between both molecules and serves as a molecular ruler (Sustarsic & Kapanidis, 2015).

Due to the high sensitivity of lumiphores and their abundance in foods, their use in assessing food status, such as identity, safety, quality and spoilage, has increased in recent years (Colaruotolo et al., 2021). These lumiphores may be intrinsic, i.e., present naturally or routinely included as an ingredient to a prepared or processed food, or extrinsic, such as synthetic probes purposely added to report on specific properties.

There are numerous components in foods that are naturally luminescent (fluorescent, phosphorescent or both) (Wolfbeis, 1985). These naturally occurring lumiphores constitute a food's autoluminescence. Table 1 compiles the most common intrinsic lumiphores encountered in fresh produce that can potentially serve as internal sensors of their state pre- and post-harvest.

Most intrinsic fluorophores in foods preferably excite in the UV and blue/green visible range (250–480 nm) of the electromagnetic spectrum, with emission maxima ranging from 280 to 700 nm. Aromatic amino acids exhibit significant FRET since the excitation and emission bands of phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr) and tryptophan (Trp) overlap (see Table 1). Hence, if all these amino acids are present in peptides or proteins, the tryptophan emission is the most easily detectable (Rossi et al., 2021). Detection of Phe emission is also limited in food samples by its low quantum yield (QY), which is one order of magnitude lower than that of Tyr and Trp (Jameson, 2014). Polyphenols are major components in plant-based foods and beverages, providing a significant source of antioxidants (Nardini, 2022). The total polyphenol content in foods and food products is highly dependent on pre and post-harvest conditions (Nardini, 2022). Most polyphenols fluoresce within the 316–480 nm range when excited between 240 and 425 nm. Besides polyphenols, fresh fruits and vegetables contain luminescent pigments that are abundant in the skins, which confer them their characteristic colours, often used as cues of their degree of ripeness and freshness. Colour, and luminescence, in these foods change due to post-harvest processes such as thermal treatments (e.g., pasteurization) and as part of the normal deteriorative processes of food.

Enzymatic browning usually occurs when polyphenol oxidases catalyze the oxidation of phenolic compounds, forming quinones, melanins and brown pigments that exhibit λex/λem bands around 320 nm/390 nm (Tilley et al., 2023). Enzymatic browning happens if a fruit is cut or injured or as part of their natural senescence. Food deterioration can be monitored based on the degradation of luminescent pigments, e.g., chlorophyll and carotenoids and the formation of various products such as chlorophyll catabolites (λex/λem = 350 nm/449 nm) or pheophytin a and b (Hernández-Sánchez et al., 2021), also luminescent.

Vitamins emit within λex/λem = 270-346 nm/326-518 nm. As it relates to the effect of pre- and post-harvest processes on fresh produce reviewed herein, riboflavin or vitamin B2, which is often present in plant-based materials, has been used to assess their quality. Riboflavin presents three absorbance bands and exhibits fluorescence at 518–525 nm and phosphorescence around 620 nm (Wang et al., 2022). However, upon thermal degradation (or photodegradation due to UV light exposure), lumichrome (λem = 444–480 nm) and lumiflavin (λem = 516–522 nm) are often formed (Radotić et al., 2023), which is characterized by a shift in the fluorescence band and a shortening of its phosphorescence average lifetime. Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) fluorescence has been utilized to assess the safety and quality of meat, eggs, fish and dairy but remains underutilized in pre and post-harvest assessment of vegetables and fruits. On the other hand, NADPH (λex/λem 330–350 nm/440-465 nm), present in cell walls, has been suggested as a potential source of information based on the blue autofluorescence seen fruits such as cherry tomatoes (Cho et al., 2013). Mycotoxins, e.g., aflatoxin B1, ochratoxin A, patulin and fuminosin, are luminescent secondary metabolites (λex/λem = 360 nm/430-520 nm) of pathogenic fungi, including but not limited to Aspergillus flavus and Fusarium spp. Chronic exposure to them leads to cancer and stunted growth in children, among others (Acković et al., 2018); hence, rapidly detecting them in grains and fruits post-harvest based on their photophysical properties can ensure food safety (Nie et al., 2021; Radotić et al., 2023). Lastly, synthetic chromophores routinely added to foods to enhance consumer appeal can be used to monitor potential fraud (e.g., organic practices) and as safe and intrinsic reporters of food integrity (Corradini et al., 2016), however as it relates to post-harvest foods, their exploration has been minimal.

Using food products' natural luminescence (e.g., autofluorescence) to monitor quality, safety and authenticity has increased in recent years (Colaruotolo et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2023). However, solely relying on intrinsic lumiphores has several limitations, e.g., low specificity and QY and short lifetimes (Colaruotolo et al., 2021) that may render them unsuitable for all applications. As such, the use of extrinsic fluorophores has been examined to improve brightness, which is related to high QY, specificity and ability to report on targeted attributes (i.e., environmental sensitivity). The addition of extrinsic fluorophores might preclude some of the advantages of using a luminescence-based approach, i.e., non-destructiveness, since their non-food-grade character will not allow for the use of the product after their addition. However, these non-edible fluorophores can help elucidate underlying reactions and mechanisms in post-harvest processes. Thus, their value cannot be disregarded. Examples of extrinsic fluorophores and their attributes are presented in Table 2 . Extrinsic fluorophores can be covalently or non-covalently attached to compounds, such as proteins or protein degradation products, for protein characterization (i.e., they may provide information on the structure, folding and unfolding, and aggregation of proteins in a sample) (Corradini & McClements, 2019). As it relates to post-harvest processes, one of the dyes within this group, Alexa Fluor 488, a non food grade synthetic fluorophore, (λex/λem = 490–499 nm/520-525 nm) (ThermoFisher, n.d.), has been applied on onion bulbs to elucidate the antibiofilm activity of mandarin peel essential oil against black mold (Aspergillus niger) (Abdel-Aziz et al., 2019). Interestingly, many synthetic dyes that double as luminescent probes are added to foods to enhance their color; however, they are toxic and prohibited from use by many countries (Corradini, 2019). Rhodamine B (RB), rhodamine 6G (R6G), and rhodamine 123 (R123) (λex/λem = 500–550 nm/520-570 nm) have been classified as illegal additives by various governing authorities, and they are only found as adulterants in foods (Chang et al., 2018). Chang et al. (2018) examined the use of excitation-emission matrices (EEMs) to detect RB, R6G and R123 added to illegally enhance the colour of chili powder. While potentially toxic as food additives, the same synthetic dyes can be used as probes for sensing metabolic processes and structural changes rather than for in-line monitoring. Jing et al. (2016) used R123's emission intensity to assess deterioration mechanisms based on the probe's ability to report on the permeability of reactive oxygen species (ROS) across mitochondrial membranes in peaches after harvesting. Similarly, FDA (fluorescein diacetate) and a boron-dipyrromethene dye (BODIPY 493/503) have been used to understand the underlying mechanisms and kinetics of membrane lipid oxidation in onions during storage (Sharma et al., 2023) and identify strategies (e.g., low doses of gamma radiation) to minimizing storage losses. Xanthene dyes such as erythrosin B (λex/λem = 500–540 nm/570 nm) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (λex/λem = 495–498 nm/517-525 nm) have been extensively operationalized as extrinsic indicators for food freshness (Chen et al., 2024; Ezati et al., 2023). As it relates to post-harvest processes and food safety, FITC has also been used to assess potential Salmonella contamination in dried fruits (Jayeola et al., 2022), and rot in sweet potatoes and cherry tomatoes (Jiang et al., 2019; Kai et al., 2021).

The development of synthetic probes and sensors has reached new levels of specificity and sensitivity with the advent of luminescent nanostructures with sizes <10 nm, such as quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), which can be used to target various elements and compounds in a sample of interest (Qu et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2015). They have been applied in the food space due to their high QY, long lifetimes, specificity and high photostability (Colaruotolo et al., 2021). Zhu et al. (2021) synthesized MoS2 QDs over multiwall carbon nanotubes for detecting postharvest fungicide residues in Chinese herbal medicines with high sensitivity. However, as QDs composition often involves heavy metals (lead or cadmium), their use in the direct monitoring of food is limited due to their potential toxicity (Qu et al., 2018). Compared to QDs, CDs have no or lower toxicity and are more environmentally friendly (Qu et al., 2018). Applications of QDs and CDs show promise for post-harvest assessment and have been applied to detect aflatoxin B1 in corn, banned synthetic food colorants in foods, herbicides in grains, evaluate antifungal efficacy of active packaging for avocados and monitor the shelf-life of foods in real-time (Ezati et al., 2022; 2023; Guo et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2015). These nanomaterials can be built as multi-mode sensors, in which luminescence sensing is complemented with other signals to enhance detection (Wei et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence-based food analysis and sensing techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence-based techniques have been widely applied for food analysis. Table 3 provides a comparison of the methods discussed below.

Traditional luminescence spectroscopy is mainly performed by exciting a sample at a selected wavelength and collecting the emission intensity at a single or a range of wavelengths, i.e., emission spectrum (Jameson, 2014). This approach has found significant applications in monitoring the quality and even safety of fresh produce at different stages of the supply chain. Its selection stems from the relatively low costs (3000 to 30,000 USD), rapid results, the possibility of implementation in-line, and low to no requirement for sample preparation. In fact, as it relates to post-harvest applications, collecting single point or luminescence spectra has been used to monitor ripening and shelf-life (Thewes et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2023), test for toxins (Gu et al., 2021) and pesticide residues (Chafiqi et al., 2023).

As foods contain multiple intrinsic fluorophores, single-spectrum methods may only allow for the assessment of a single compound at a time (Colaruotolo et al., 2021). Excitation-emission matrices (EEMs) allow for the simultaneous measurement of the emission intensity of multiple lumiphores in the sample as a function of both excitation and emission wavelengths (Liu et al., 2019). Thus, EEMs provide a fluorescence fingerprint of a sample and reflect its autofluorescence, which better characterizes the attributes of a food product (Colaruotolo et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019). Changes in peak intensity, shifts in fluorescence maxima, or broadening of multiple fluorescence peaks often correlate with changes in the integrity of a sample. EEM collection is also non-invasive, rapid (as full EEMs can be collected in a few minutes if instruments equipped with CCD cameras are used), does not require significant sample preparation and requires investments of 20,000 to 65,000 USD depending on the level of resolution needed. One of the first applications of EEMs in post-harvest was conducted by Zhu et al. (2007) to determine carbendazim in bananas. Since then, this method has gained traction in the literature for food safety and quality assessment, particularly for the classification and authentication of juices (Sikorska et al., 2022), wines (Ranaweera et al., 2024), oils (Lia et al., 2020), and vinegar (Ríos-Reina et al., 2021).

However, while EEMs are able to analyze both liquid and solid foods, optimization is required depending on the sample matrix. For example, light scattering in solid, semi-solid and opaque foods may adversely distort or mask fluorescence signal detection. Hence, samples of such foods might require more extensive preparation (e.g., homogenization, extraction, dilution) than those of liquid specimens prior to testing. Alternatively, data collection in front-face mode can facilitate luminescence sensing on solid and semi-solids; however, the collected data will only reflect the properties of the sample's surface. Hence, the recorded data might not be representative of heterogeneous samples. Using this EEM modality, Bartolić et al. (2022) examined the effects of UV-irradiation on the antioxidative properties of soybean seeds. Similarly, Sarimov et al. (2021) used front-face EEMs to scout apples and potatoes for disease or damage.

Spectral measurements alone provide limited information related to state (quality, safety, progress of biochemical reactions) in heterogeneous matrices such as foods. Luminescence-based imaging techniques provide both spatial and spectral domain resolution, which allows for a better understanding of quality changes or decay initiation, progress, and distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microspectroscopy principles and potential uses in fresh produce and derived products
ce:section.ce:para: Non-luminescent components and microstructure may impact the progress of reactions and processes leading to ripening and spoilage. Thus, imaging the distribution of lumiphores and their photophysical properties within vegetal tissues contributes to gaining insights into, for example, ripening and deterioration mechanisms and processes.

Conventional fluorescence microscopy can leverage a food's autofluorescence due to the presence of any of the natural lumiphores listed in Table 1. However, most often, synthetic fluorescent dyes are preferred in microscopy because of their higher quantum yields, brightness, selective interactions with specific food components and their better-defined and narrower environmental sensitivity (i.e., their ability to report on the physical and chemical properties of the surrounding medium). The availability of a tunable light source or several lasers allows for exciting the sample at selected wavelengths, easily distinguishing the emission from the non-emitting background.

As it relates to fresh produce and post-harvest processing, wide-field fluorescence microscopy, (WFFM) and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) have been the methods most often applied. WFFM allows acquiring a 2D image of the entire sample, capturing the image depth, regardless of potentially out-of-focus structures. Depending on the resolution and function required, WFM systems cost 2,500 to 25,000 USD. Unlike WFFM, where the whole specimen is evenly illuminated, LSCM uses pinhole apertures to illuminate a specific point within the xyz-space. Hence, only a portion of the sample is illuminated at a time, which enhances contrast and minimizes out-of-focus blur. This setup enables the sample to be sectioned into thin optical layers along the z-axis without disturbing its internal structure so each layer can be analyzed individually or stacked together for 3D reconstruction, providing valuable insights into its actual structure (Corradini & McClements, 2019). Due to enhanced imaging capabilities, LSCM systems usually cost more (e.g., 50,000 to a few hundred thousand dollars) than WFMs. Most LSCM techniques rely on compound-specific fluorescent probes or fluorescent-tagged immunolabels, allowing simultaneous visualization of the presence, state, and spatial organization of components like lipids, proteins, and even bacterial colonies within a food sample. Fluorescent labels used in WFFM are also suitable for LSCM. Although targeting specific compounds, e.g., lipids or proteins, with compatible fluorophores facilitates assessing structures and component distribution, sample preparation requirements limit their applicability (Sharma et al., 2023).

WFFM has been more commonly used than LSCM for post-harvest applications due to relatively quick scans and the ability to image live cells to elucidate antimicrobial mechanisms, distribution and attachment of mold or bacteria, wound healing and enzymatic browning/cell death (Jayeola et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2012; Wang, Niu, et al., 2019). LSCM's lower detector efficiency and slower acquisition speed make it less suitable for live-cell imaging (FocalPlane, 2021). Despite its limitations for rapid testing, recent advances in LSCM have increased its application in the food space. Hence, LSCM was used to examine cell membrane activity, such as the generation and quenching of reactive oxygen species (ROS), distribution of post-harvest diseases, or ripening processes. For example, Kai et al. (2021) monitored the accumulation of ROS in the epidermal peels of cherry tomatoes using LSCM, identifying the role of chlorogenic acid in potentially delaying tissue decay, specifically against Fusarium rot.

The reduction in the cost of detectors that can effectively monitor concentration-independent photophysical properties, i.e., lifetimes, has advanced the application of Fluorescence or Phosphorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM or PLIM, respectively) in biological and food applications (Jameson, 2014). WFM and LSCM can be equipped with FLIM and PLIM capabilities; however, their applications to plant-based commodities pre and post-harvest are limited, which may be related to their operational requirements (Colaruotolo et al., 2021) and cost, which is usually in the range of 200,000 to 750,000 USD. Despite these limitations, some applications have been reported. Kim et al. (2008) used a time-resolved multispectral laser-induced fluorescence lifetime imaging system to test for animal feces on apples. While the study did not attain microscopic resolution, it showed the potential of FLIM for product monitoring along the supply chain, particularly in the post-harvest space.

Fluorescence microscopy is limited by the diffraction limit of the light due to spatial resolution and magnification. For this reason, Super Resolution Fluorescence Microscopy (SRFM) has been proposed as it allows for the assessment of microstructures and molecules, providing higher-resolution images compared to confocal microscopy (<50 nm) (Gallegos-Cerda et al., 2023). Utilizing novel, purposely designed fluorophores can help overcome the diffraction barrier and improve specificity (Gallegos-Cerda et al., 2023). Despite its advantages, SRFM is rarely used in foods, with very few to no applications related to post-harvest processing, which may be partly due to its high cost (500,000–1,000,000 USD). Nonetheless, SRFM has significant potential. As such, it is expected that its use to assess protein, lipid and carbohydrate distribution, structure and pathways/reactions (Gallegos-Cerda et al., 2023) will increase in the future.

In recent years, machine vision systems have gained traction due to their potential to collect post-harvest quality and safety monitoring data, as well as to assist with image classification during harvesting and packaging and for detecting and grading potential post-harvest products (Momin et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2024). Spectral imaging, such as multispectral (up to 10 bands) and hyperspectral (1–20 nm resolution) imaging, allows combining imaging and spectroscopy (Momin et al., 2023). Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) has been primarily applied under reflectance or transmittance modes within ranges from the visible to short-wave infrared wavelength or UV to visible wavelengths when coupled with specialized optic devices (e.g., shutters, long-wave filters) (Lu et al., 2020). When spectral and spatial information is required, HSI is more applicable and provides more details than EEMs (Cho et al., 2013; Momin et al., 2023). HSI integrates multispectral analysis of intrinsic fluorophores with spatial data from imaging methods, pixel by pixel, subsequently providing safety and quality information, in real-time, for a food of interest (Cho et al., 2013). In fact, HSI has been reported to perform better than reflectance imaging for measuring materials with smooth surfaces (Cho et al., 2013). As such, HSI in fluorescence mode has been used to differentiate between mature and immature fruits and vegetables, detect surface damage or defects, and assess sanitation, contamination and presence of residues on the surfaces of leaves, vegetables, fresh and dried fruit (Fatchurrahman et al., 2020). It should be noted, however, that HSI only provides a surface probing approach, reaching mm-depth under the produce skin, as emitted light from deeper regions may be quenched or re-absorbed and limited penetrationis attained due to the wavelengths utilized. Regardless of the luminescence technique applied, a common outcome of all of them is the density of the data obtained, which requires extensive analysis to be appropriately integrated into monitoring tools. HSI in fluorescence mode requires a camera and spectrometer that cover the UV-visible range, of which a customized system could cost 20,000 to 100,000 USD. Although most of the examples provided refer to method development and thus cover research tools, the applicability of such techniques commercially is growing, as has been summarized by Ma et al. (2023).

The interpretation and applicability of the sheer amount of data generated by luminescence spectroscopy techniques require the use of suitable analysis techniques for data dimensionality reduction, classification and discrimination, as well as quantitative prediction. As such, chemometric methods, including multivariate analysis and machine learning algorithms, have been studied and applied (Sádecká & Tóthová, 2007). These techniques can be subdivided into “non-supervised” and “supervised,” as shown in Fig. 1 . The specific applications, strengths and limitations of selected techniques that are commonly used in the analysis of luminescence sensing data in pre- and post-harvest are summarized in Table 4 and discussed further herein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unsupervised approaches applied to luminescence data of fresh and processed produce
ce:section.ce:para: Most luminescence data is unfolded and arranged into matrices, with each row representing the sample and each column representing the variable (i.e., the excitation and emission wavelengths or intensity) (Sikorska et al., 2019) before further analysis. PCA is one of the most commonly used variable-reduction methods, particularly for datasets with high dimensionality (Ahmad et al., 2017). During PCA, original predictor variables are centered, linearly combined, and then converted to a new set of variables (known as the principal components, PCs) based on the variables scores and the loadings, which are more descriptive, allowing for the identification of commonalities between samples based on their main attributes. As a result, the dimensionality of the original spectral data is significantly reduced, requiring less computing power and the samples are arranged into clusters, generating a similarity map (Sádecká & Tóthová, 2007). Sarimov et al. (2021) used fluorescence spectra, specifically intensity and energy, in combination with PCA to effectively discriminate between healthy and diseased apples and potatoes. The bands obtained at λex/λem = 300–350 nm/400-450 nm and λex/λem = 400–450 nm/680-750 nm differed in healthy and scabbed apples, with the former also indicating potato rot (Sarimov et al., 2021). PCA has also been used for clustering samples post-harvest to indicate contamination, spoilage and progress of metabolic processes by analyzing the fluorescence spectra of peanuts for aflatoxin contamination (Gu et al., 2021), HSI data of fresh-cut peppers (Delwiche et al., 2019) for remaining shelf-life, and the increase in fluorescence intensity of chlorophyll as a function of respiration in stored apples (Thewes et al., 2021). Despite its extensive applications, PCA has multiple limitations. Firstly, PCA relies on the bilinear transformation of original variables; thus, it is not robust enough to capture nonlinear correlations. Additionally, variables in the PC domain do not directly reflect the meaning of the original data, which requires additional effort to interpret the results. Conversely, PARAFAC is a trilinear technique that decomposes three-way or high-order data arrays into 1st order ones, making it particularly suitable for EEM data that cannot be adequately handled using PCA (Acković et al., 2018, pp. 161–197). There is no rotational freedom in PARAFAC. Therefore, while it is challenging to discern the pure underlying components or latent variables using PCA, PARAFAC allows for a proper understanding of the components obtained by recording the EEMs of a sample (Acković et al., 2018; Sádecká & Tóthová, 2007). For this reason, PARAFAC has been predominantly proposed and applied to analyze EEMs of fruits, cereals, juices and wines to detect food adulteration (Acković et al., 2018; Cabrera-Bañegil et al., 2020). For example, Cabrera-Bañegil et al. (2020) used PARAFAC on EEM data to assess differences in polyphenol content in the skins of Japanese plums based on harvesting time/season. Catechin, epicatechin, chlorogenic acid and nonchlorogenic acid content in plums decreased as the harvesting season progressed (from May to September) and were lower in the pulp than the skin. Similarly, Schueuermann et al. (2018) used PARAFAC to process EEMs of grape juices and wines and examined their quality as a function of their growing location within a vineyard and during the winemaking process (pre-fermentation, pre-barrelling, barrelled wines). As in the study conducted by Singh et al. (2025) on maple syrup authenticity based on EEMs, the main PARAFAC components in grapes and wines corresponded to tryptophan, hydroxyl benzoic acid (clone discrimination) and catechin and were the most relevant fluorophores for quality assessment in these products. PARAFAC on EEMs of jams and syrups have also allowed spotting the addition of colorants in these products (Rubio et al., 2019a; b), showing its potential in assessing the use of illegal dyes and colorings in foods.

In terms of HSI postharvest data analysis, Successive Projection Algorithms (SPA) constitute a robust and widely used method for feature extraction and data dimension reduction (Sun et al., 2022). Compared to PCA, SPA does not rely on variable transformation and can reduce concerns regarding multicollinearity while maintaining the original data domain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supervised techniques applied to luminescence data of fresh and processed produce
ce:section.ce:para: Supervised chemometric techniques require additional knowledge of the sample, such as the potential outcomes that a set can exhibit; thus, they are often used for classification or regression purposes. In luminescence-based testing, these techniques are used to correlate and assign spectra or bands to specific classes/groups for discrimination (Sádecká & Tóthová, 2007). Advances in computational speed and power, as well as increased availability of tools and packages to aid in coding, have allowed the broad application of machine learning or artificial intelligence algorithms in luminescence sensing in pre- and post-harvest scenarios, particularly when dealing with large, complex datasets (Zhu et al., 2021).

Regression analyses are applied to reduce collinearity and dataset size prior to discriminatory analysis (Sádecká & Tóthová, 2007). Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) allows for classification and dimensionality reduction and can be used for authentication analysis for fruit and vegetable products (Esteki et al., 2018). LDA is often used on images, which contain a large number of features that need to be trimmed down prior to classification (Gambella et al., 2021). While the goal of both LDA and PCA is dimensionality reduction, LDA is more robust than PCA as it accounts for the labelled data when reporting on the projection matrix (Gambella et al., 2021). However, LDA assumes data normality and linearity, which may not always hold with smaller datasets.

Multi-linear regression (MLR) is one of the traditional approaches to quantitatively correlate a response variable with multiple predictor variables. However, MLR requires a large data size for robustness and is vulnerable to data collinearity, a feature prevalent in spectral data, and overfitting (Cheng and Sun, 2015; Benoudjit et al., 2009).

Partial least squares regression (PLSR) is widely used in pre- and post-harvest applications. PLSR reduces data dimensionality by extracting the latent factors, i.e., those with the highest variability (Tobias, 2016). PLSR is suitable for more accurate prediction of response variables rather than understanding the undelaying relationships between variables. PLSR has been applied to luminescent data for fungal and toxin detection, assessment and prediction of produce maturity and classification of plant cultivars (Armstrong et al., 2023; Birenboim et al., 2023; Gu et al., 2021).

Among the supervised algorithms, K-nearest neighbors (KNN), support vector machines (SVM) and random forest (RF) are the most frequently reported for the analysis of spectral data in produce. KNN divides the data into pre-set clusters based on the distance between the values of different features (Zhu et al., 2021). The closer the values that a sample's attribute/s exhibit in relation to a reference point, the greater the likelihood that that sample belongs to a particular category or class (Zhu et al., 2021). Ebersbach et al. (2018) used KNN for the analysis of HSI data to classify cannabis varieties into high and low producers of bioactive compounds, as this data analysis technique better represented the nonlinear nature of the data rather than using a linear unmixing model. This analysis allowed differentiating cell types based on secondary metabolite content while also identifying growth conditions leading to better performance. SVMs are primarily used for classification but can also perform regression tasks, in which case they are dubbed support vector regression (SVR) (Gandhi, 2018). The goal of SVM is to find a hyperplane that allows for classification in the N-dimensional space (where N represents the number of features) to uniquely represent the sample data points (Gandhi, 2018). SVM is effective even if the number of dimensions is similar to the sample number, efficient in memory and versatile; however, overfitting may occur (Scikit, 2024). Zheng et al. (2010) used SVM to classify jujube fruits based on post-harvest quality based on chlorophyll fluorescence emission intensity during photosynthesis and minimum and maximum respiration rates collected using fiber optics, achieving 87–93 % correct classification depending on the type of classifier used, e.g., RBF (radical basis function), sigmoidal or polynomial SVMs. The study identified correlations between photophysical parameters that allowed gaining insights into the degree of ripeness of jujube in real-time. More recently, Bertani et al. (2020) also used SVM to analyze fluorescence spectra of almond slurry to detect potential aflatoxin contamination, achieving 95 % classification accuracy.

RF is another supervised learning method often used in pre- and postharvest applications. This approach applies multiple decision trees generated with different data subsets randomly selected during the training set to reach a single output. This technique reduces the susceptibility to overfitting while also increasing prediction accuracy (IBM, n.d.). Each decision tree in the RF operates independently, with the final result being determined by the most frequent result from all classification outcomes (Zhu et al., 2021). Fu and Wang (2022) used SVMs and RF to classify pears based on their early stages of physical damage, i.e., level of bruising, as detected using HSI. While SVM provided a high classification accuracy (87–97 %, depending on the preprocessing methods used), RF performed better when multivariate scatter correction was used. However, RF can be susceptible to overfitting, particularly in systems with class imbalances. Extreme Gradient Boosting, or XGBoost, is superior for unbalanced datasets as it gives priority to underrepresented classes by customizing its learning process (Fatima et al., 2023). For example, XGBoost has been applied to assess EEM data on the maturity of Cabernet Sauvignon grapes with 78 % correct classification (Armstrong et al., 2023).

Artificial neural networks (ANN) refers to a collection of connected units or nodes, i.e., neurons, that allow establishing relationships among different input variables using defined functions so that a path for obtaining a particular output can be effectively determined. ANN structure is typically composed of an input layer, which provides the numerical data of the featured sets, one or multiple hidden layers, which establish the relationships and computations and an output layer, which comprises the potential or actual outcomes (Zhang et al., 2022). During training, the weights are adjusted so that the differences between the actual and estimated outputs are minimized. ANN with backpropagation algorithms (BP-ANN) has been the most common method used for analyzing pre- and post-harvest spectroscopic and HSI data. BP-ANN uses the error rate or loss from a previous epoch or iteration to fine-tune the weights of the ANN. For example, Ariana et al. (2006) analyzed multispectral and fluorescence images using BP-ANN, pixel by pixel, to classify apples into those with normal tissue and those with tissue disorders as well as identifying varieties (i.e., Honeycrisp, Red Delicious and Redcort). Similarly, Li et al. (2022) combined fluorescence images and reflectance HSI, analyzed using PLS-DA and BP-ANN to detect early decay in oranges and classify them as fresh or spoilt with 97 % accuracy.

Convolutional neural networks (CNN) refer to a deep learning technique increasingly gaining popularity due to advancements in computing power. CNN allows for feature extraction by using convolutional features as filters, primarily for the analysis of images or for computer vision tasks (Sadimantara et al., 2024), with the output representing a subsample of the pooled layers. Itakura et al. (2019) used CNN to analyze EEM data to estimate citrus fruit maturity. This study considered each collected EEM as an image, and CNN was applied in conjunction with total solid solutes expressed as °Brix and acidity values to estimate the °Brix/acid ratio in citrus fruits. The absolute error of the CNN model (2.48) was significantly better than other tested methods from a prior study that examined only the bands corresponding to polymethoxyflavone (6.21), tryptophan and chlorophyll (4.49) and PCR (4.04) (Itakura et al., 2019; Muharfiza et al., 2017). CNN has also been used to evaluate pesticide residues on citrus leaves using computer vision techniques with 395 nm illumination (Basavaraju et al., 2024). Similarly, Sadimantara et al. (2024) identified aflatoxin contamination in cocoa beans with an accuracy of 96 % based on fluorescence images using CNN. Hu et al. (2025) and Singh et al. (2025) applied CNN to analyze EEMs of vegetable oils such as camellia oil and maple syrup to assess their authenticity. Both studies found that the CNN model outperformed other tested models, such as KNN, SVM and PLSDA, in accurately classifying samples. Despite the ability of CNN to improve the interpretation and utilization of EEMs and fluorescence images in produce, this approach is not widespread since it often requires high computational power and large sample sizes to deliver accurate and fast predictions. Hence, several of the unsupervised and supervised techniques introduced in this section and Table 4 are usually used as data preprocessing methods before inputting them into a CNN model to minimize prediction bias and errors. Additionally, methods to enhance sample size, such as adding simulated randomized (e.g., ± 1 point up or down) data points to the dataset, allow for achieving larger data sets for training, testing and validation, without incurring time-consuming or expensive data collection. However, this practice should be used with caution since it may result in skewing of the outcomes and overfitting.

Furthermore, while using these methods individually can provide high accuracy, using multiple learning models for classification, or ensemble learning, can improve accuracy and make more robust predictions by leveraging the strengths and overcoming the weaknesses of the individual models. However, as it relates to luminescence sensing during pre- and post-harvest of produce, the practicality of a combined approach for in-line monitoring should be considered and needs further investigation, which is currently limited by the lack of large enough and accurately labeled datasets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of luminescence techniques in post-harvest safety and quality of fresh produce and products
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence-based techniques show great potential for sensing and reporting on food quality and safety at multiple stages throughout the food supply chain. This section summarizes the most relevant applications of luminescence sensing techniques to assist in fresh produce sorting/classification at the early stages of the supply chain, evaluate the effect of processing on food quality in the later stages, and detect food safety risks and monitor product authenticity throughout as shown in Fig. 2 . It is essential to note that a single method or procedure might serve multiple purposes, emphasizing the capability of these techniques to assess aspects related to quality, safety and identity of produce at the same time. For example, sorting a sample by its maturity also allows for gaining insights into its shelf-life. Authenticity may also be associated with safety risk detection or processing effects. Hence, in the following sections, these aspects are discussed in conjunction when appropriate.

Food safety has direct impacts on consumer health and overall public safety. Thus, it constitutes a central tenet of the integrity of the food supply. Contaminated foods may result in foodborne outbreaks or chronic illnesses, particularly affecting vulnerable populations. Contamination may occur due to the introduction of microorganisms such as bacteria (e.g., Salmonella enteritidis, Escherichia coli O157:H7), parasites or the formation of toxins. As such, traceability is essential to ensure the safety, health and well-being of consumers. Mycotoxins are secondary metabolites of fungi and have been reported to contaminate numerous foods and feed, such as nuts, milk powder, wheat, and corn/maize, with the prevalence of these toxins increasing due to the high incidence of extreme climate events (Casu et al., 2024). As early as 2008, Hruska et al. (2008) used HSI in fluorescence mode to monitor aflatoxin production in maize inoculated with two different toxigenic strains. Inoculated and naturally infected corn showed a fluorescence band at 501 nm, which was absent in non-contaminated samples. More recently, Gu et al. (2021) discriminated between peanut kernels infected with aflatoxigenic and non-aflatoxigenic fungal species using fluorescence spectroscopy (λex/λem = 360 nm/380-620 nm) in tandem with headspace-gas chromatography-ion mobility spectrometry (HS-GC-IMS) fingerprinting. The data was analyzed using PCA, PLS and data fusion strategies at three levels, namely data, the first principal component and features based on variable importance. As a result, fluorescence emission from 415 to 440 nm (λex = 360 nm) provided a reliable output to identify infected peanuts. To enhance discrimination between infected and non-infected peanuts, the intensity of the most characteristic bands (at 415–440 nm and 500–510 nm) was correlated with fungal species, such as aflatoxin B1, so that a 97 % correct classification was achieved.

Fruits and vegetables may be exposed to chemical or microbial contamination through soil or water during pre- and post-harvest steps, including but not limited to irrigation, cooling or handling. In fact, pathogenic bacteria associated with animal or human fecal contamination can come into contact with crops due to livestock, wildlife or even proximity to human waste systems. The risk for foodborne outbreaks having fresh fruit and vegetables as carriers of the etiological agent, i.e., the pathogenic microorganism or chemical contaminant, is at its highest since these foods are often consumed raw or after only minimal processing (Lefcourt & Siemens, 2017). Hence, Lefcourt and Siemens (2017) applied HSI and a CCD camera to reduce data acquisition time to detect spinach leaves contaminated with fecal matter and infer its accompanying risks. This study revealed that surveying fields at night results in more reliable data acquisition than in full daylight when a 525 nm interference filter was used. It is, however, essential to note that HSI has limitations in quantifying very low concentrations of foodborne pathogens, as such microbial densities higher than 102 CFU/g are often required for accurate assessment (Bonah et al., 2019). Mo et al. (2017) also used HSI in fluorescence mode to detect worms on fresh-cut lettuce with a 99 % accuracy, indicating the potential of this approach for rapid detection of pests in post-harvest produce.

While pre-harvest contamination constitutes a significant risk for fresh fruit and vegetables, steps involved in the minimal processing of these commodities, particularly peeling and cutting, make them highly vulnerable to pathogen contamination. Additionally, the removal of protective layers and the increase in surface area generated by these processes raise the risk for contamination and microbial growth (Hwang et al., 2021). In fact, contamination or cross-contamination of food from processing and contact surfaces (including equipment) is one of the major sources of pathogen transfer to food products. Also, food residues or debris that remain on surfaces due to improper cleaning may create an environment for biofilm formation by harboring and supporting microbial growth. Camera-based fluorescence imaging provides a quick and sensitive method for detecting remaining food residues (with an accuracy >95 %), sources of contamination, and sanitation of pathogens (>99.99 %) on food contact surfaces (Hwang et al., 2021; Sueker et al., 2021). Hwang et al. (2021) used HSI to detect fruit residues in contact surfaces and equipment after processing. Broad fluorescence emission bands within the 450–600 nm and 600–730 nm wavelengths (λex = 365 nm) signalled the presence of honeydew melon, orange, apple or watermelon debris and microbial contamination due to inadequate cleaning/sanitation with a detection accuracy of 95–100 %. To enhance the applicability of this approach, Sueker et al. (2021) developed a handheld fluorescence imaging system using two excitation wavelengths, i.e., 275 nm and 405 nm, to provide rapid detection of respiratory droplets, saliva and other organic residues that may be present on food contact surfaces. The system also includes documentation, i.e., image database of contamination records, on the type of residue or contaminants on surfaces and disinfects the area. As such, this system can act as a food processing auditing and corrective tool, providing rapid and accurate in-line monitoring and adequate sanitation by exposing the surfaces identified as contaminated to UV-C light (Sueker et al., 2021).

Pesticides, including insecticides, antimicrobial agents, fungicides, and herbicides, are often applied to foods pre-harvest to prevent or eliminate unwanted pests, bacteria, fungi and weeds. Additionally, climate change has significantly increased pest and disease stresses on crops. As such, fresh fruits and vegetables are among the primary sources of exposure to pesticides, which may be toxic to consumers, causing significant health issues (Chafiqi et al., 2023). Pesticides and antimicrobial agents are required to increase yields, reduce contamination levels and extend shelf-life, but they must degrade or be removed from produce prior to consumption. Fortunately, synthetic pesticides and antimicrobial agents usually contain aromatic rings or other moieties in their molecular structure that make them luminescent, which provides the opportunity to use luminescence-based sensing techniques to evaluate their residues. Chafiqi et al. (2023) assessed the effectiveness of ultrasonic cleaning of lettuce to remove pesticide residues based on EEMs and PARAFAC, confirming the potential of EEMs for in-line monitoring of pesticides (boscalid and pyraclostrobin) in these products during and after processes. HSI applicability for in-line pesticide residue detection (up to 500x diluted bifenthrin, diafenthiuron, tolfenpyrad and imidacloprid) was reported by Sun et al. (2022), who examined their persistence in black tea. The study applied multiple machine learning methods, particularly CNN-RF, for rapid and accurate (99 %) detection. It is important to note, however, that luminescence-based methods such as HSI are only able to detect the presence or absence of residues and may not be able to differentiate between or among various pesticides. More involved analytical methods, such as LC-MS/MS, may be required to univocally ascertain the correspondence of an emission band with a particular compound, i.e., the type of pesticide. As such, luminescence-based methods may be used for initial screening and detection of contamination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freshness, ripeness and presence/absence of defects
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most critical factors in determining the quality of fruits is maturity, or their final stage of growth and development. Harvest time may affect a fruit's metabolic processes, ripening rate and, subsequently, shelf-life. Harvesting citrus fruits too early may preclude further ripening and result in consumer rejection and, thus, unmarketability. Conversely, harvesting mature or overripe citrus fruits renders them increasingly susceptible to physical damage, which may accelerate spoilage or rotting (Muharfiza et al., 2017). Muharfiza et al. (2017) used EEMs to scout Japanese mandarin (Citrus unshiu) for maturity markers. These authors identified valuable relationships between the photophysical properties of the main fluorophores in this crop (chlorophyll a and b, flavonoids, coumarin and tryptophan-like compounds) and maturity levels. The ratio between the maximum fluorescence intensity of aromatic amino acids (λex/λem = 260 nm/330 nm) and chlorophyll a (λex/λem = 410 nm/675 nm) significantly correlated with the total soluble solids-to-acid ratio, which constitutes a maturity index.

To ensure food quality and compliance with regulatory standards, food is often inspected and sorted based on its size, colour, shape, ripeness or maturity, textural or sensorial properties and presence of disease and defects (CFIA, 2023; Momin et al., 2023). As such, establishing grades for food is not only crucial for quality but ensures that consumer expectations are met, establishes price points for high and low-value products (CFIA, 2023), and provides guidance to producers on fruit and vegetable uses and repurposing, such as fruit into juices or fermentation in the liquor industry. Cabrera-Bañegil et al. (2019) used LDA-PARAFAC to analyze the EEMs of grapes to assess their maturity. Upon excitation at 280 nm, the emission bands at 310–340 nm provided valuable information to evaluate grape ripeness levels. These bands correspond to various fluorophores, i.e., amino acids and monomeric catechins, which decreased during ripening (Cabrera-Bañegil et al., 2019). Irrigation also affected the maturity of grapes, as revealed by changes in the resveratrol band, which increased upon ripening (Cabrera-Bañegil et al., 2019). This approach allowed for 96–100 % correct classification of grapes based on their maturity and irrigation practices. Similarly, Zhao et al. (2023) studied the post-harvest ripening of two varieties of corn by examining the fluorescence intensity of tyrosine (λex/λem = 280 nm/305-310 nm), the primary aromatic amino acid in zein, over 56 days of storage at 15 and 25 °C, which allowed elucidating the role of corn variety and storage temperature on the ripening process. More recently, Armstrong et al. (2023) used absorbance, transmission and fluorescence EEMs (A-TEEM) in combination with PLS to evaluate the phenolic content, flavor and ripeness of Cabernet Sauvignon grapes grown under different conditions and viticulture techniques. The results allowed for the identification of the main factors that determine harvest date while also providing producers with information to optimize harvesting times and conditions based on maturity.

Lastly, assessment of damage, defects, or disease can allow grading and discrimination in fruits and vegetables. For example, Chiu et al. (2015) used chlorophyll fluorescence emission in Golden Delicious apples upon photoexcitation at 470 nm as detected with a CCD camera to identify mechanical damage. The study identified damage with an 80–90 % accuracy, depending on the force of the mechanical impact, after as little as 30 min. Regardless of impact force, bruise detection increased to 100 % after 1h. As such, this study shows the applications of fluorescence and imaging for in-line and on-line monitoring of damage and defects in fresh produce.

The collection of fluorescence emission intensity and lifetimes may also facilitate the detection of food fraud or adulteration. Food fraud is a multi-billion dollar deception practice that refers to the undeclared addition, removal or misrepresentation of food or ingredients for economic gain. Raman, mid and near-infrared (IR) spectroscopic and chromatographic methods have been historically and extensively applied to detect adulteration in foods such as juices, oils and powders (Arendse et al., 2021). However, in recent years, traditional and novel fluorescence-based methods for adulteration assessment of produce at postharvest stages have gained traction. Ammari et al. (2015) analyzed the EEMs of orange and grapefruit juices using independent component analysis (ICA) and MLR. The study revealed different EEMs for orange and grapefruit juices that allowed detecting grapefruit adulteration in orange juice at levels as low as 1 %. Sabir et al. (2024) identified the adulteration of mango juices with artificial colours based on the spectra collected upon excitation at 405 nm. Emission bands corresponding to chlorophyll (674 and 772 nm) and beta carotene (580 and 634 nm), followed by classification using PCA, allowed for the detection of commercial mango juice samples produced with unripened fruits whose color was “corrected” using undeclared dyes. Han et al. (2024) evaluated the capability of FLIM to characterize the origin of powdered spices, specifically ginger, based on the distribution and presence of its inherent fluorophores, i.e., its primary pigment, curcumin. This approach revealed that grinding to different particle sizes did not affect the discriminatory power of the lifetime measurements, providing a superior tool to the currently used to detect adulteration in powders.

As it pertains to wine, food fraud or adulteration may occur by misrepresenting geographic origin, age or grape variety (Singh, 2020). Ranaweera et al. (2024) examined the use of absorbance and transmission excitation-emission matrix (A-TEEM) for the assessment and discrimination of Cabernet Sauvignon wines based on geographical origin. Additionally, using machine learning to analyze A-TEEM data improved the estimation of phenolic compounds content in red wines, which in turn may reveal their geographical origin. Ranaweera et al. (2024) used PARAFAC to decompose the EEMs data and found 5 components mainly responsible for wine fluorescence: 1) λex/λem = 278 nm/318 nm, 2) λex/λem = 286 nm/352 nm, 3) λex/λem = 286 nm/316 nm and 4) λex/λem = 263 nm/400 nm, potentially associated with catechin, anthocyanins, epicatechin and to phenolic acid, respectively and a broad fluorescent region at 5) λex/λem = 260–400 nm/380-500 nm, which may be due to vitamins or the interactions of various phenolic compounds. Using XGBDA, wine variety and geographical origin could be appropriately classified with an accuracy of 99.7 % and 100 %, respectively. EEMs could also be used to detect the presence of synthetic food colorants such as cochineal (E−120) (λex/λem = 495 nm/553 nm) and erythrosine (E−127) (λex/λem = 510 nm/553 nm) when illegally added to food products such as cherries in syrup and strawberry jams (Rubio et al., 2019a; b). The concentrations found by analyzing the fluorescence data were comparable to those obtained by HPLC-DAD.

Preserving and extending the shelf life of fresh produce requires various processing methods, starting from cooling upon harvesting to cleaning and sorting and more involved unit operations such as pasteurization, fermentation, drying and freezing. The selection of the process depends on the food product in question, the quality attributes that are more desirable to maintain and the subsequent commercialization. Among the principal quality attributes, preserving the visual, flavor and texture of the product often comes first, and their decay cannot always be associated with the progress of chemical reactions. For example, Brettanomyces bruxellensis is one of the major contributors to wine spoilage, producing off-flavours during the fermentation process (Chandra et al., 2016). Chandra et al. (2016) used WFM to assess the growth of B. bruxellensis during fermentation and correlate it with the content of 4-ethylphenol, the compound suspected of causing off-flavours, after incubation.

Additionally, microorganisms may remain on the skin of fruit even after processing, which may constitute a food safety risk; hence, assessing a process’ efficacy is of utmost importance. Hot-water treatments (HWT) are often applied to fresh-cut fruit; however, the optimal conditions to maintain product quality while inactivating spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms should be identified (Herppich et al., 2020). Herppich et al. (2020) used chlorophyll fluorescence imaging (CFI) on HWT apples treated from 44 to 70 °C for 30 to 300s and stored for 7 days at 4 °C. The study found that depending on the cultivar of apples, they could be HWT at 55–60 °C for 30–120s without impacting their chlorophyll or anthocyanin content, subsequently maintaining the fruit quality while providing a decontamination step (Herppich et al., 2020). Schlüter et al. (2009) also used CFI with a CCD camera to study the effects of high-hydrostatic pressure on chlorophyll concentration in lettuce and found that pressures below 124 MPa or temperatures below 45 °C did not irreversibly damage the leaf tissue. Treatment of fresh produce with ozone, O3, has been reported to protect fruit quality during storage due to its bactericidal and fungicidal effects triggered by damaging microbial cell membranes of microorganisms (Piechowiak et al., 2019). Piechowiak et al. (2019) monitored vitamin C content in blueberries, untreated and treated with ozone at 15 ppm every 12 h for 28 days using EEMs, the latter showing less vitamin C losses than the control over time and preventing microbial growth (Piechowiak et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidation of metabolic pathways and mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: Fruits and vegetables continue to ripen or mature after harvesting, leading to short shelf lives and significant post-harvest losses. Proper storage and handling of these foods are crucial for extending their shelf-life; however, various foods may have different storage requirements and mechanisms that delay or prolong ripeness. Vegetables and fruits are susceptible to cold stress injury upon storage, which presents a significant concern in the post-harvest or post-production phase (Peng et al., 2021). FLIM has been used for injury assessment of plants by examining chlorophyll autofluorescence, detecting cold stress, particularly changes induced by freeze-thaw cycles related to the formation of intra and extra-cellular ice crystals and lipid rearrangements at the chloroplast membrane (Peng et al., 2021). Additionally, FLIM can be used to image and track the cellular microenvironment, allowing for the imaging of bioactive formation, transport and uptake (Chen et al., 2023), thus gaining insights into the mechanisms behind senesce and responses to stressors.

Similarly, fluorescence spectroscopy allowed examining the efficiency of limonene liposomes on strawberry quality over 14 days of storage (Dhital et al., 2018). Applications of this essential oil encapsulated in edible nano-coatings allowed for maintaining food quality attributes and extending shelf life by preventing nutrient loss and microbial growth. Limonene-coated strawberries preserved post-harvest quality attributes better than untreated ones, as reflected by their higher anthocyanin content (Dhital et al., 2018). FLIM has also been used to study defects and inability to reproduce in commercially cultivated garlic (Tchórzewska et al., 2018). FLIM detected metabolic discrepancies in the tapetal cells that hinder nutrient absorption behind the epidermis and endodermis. This disruption in tapetal development has been linked to cell death and was indicated as the leading cause of sterility in garlic (Tchórzewska et al., 2018).

Luminescence-based spectrometric analyses could also be used to collect data related to the quality and safety changes of foods due to various pre- and postharvest processes. The dataset can assist in developing mechanistic kinetic models for quality and safety parameters or processes in selected foods based on fundamental physical, chemical and biological principles (Faieta et al., 2024). Within the ranges of conditions of model development, these mechanistic models can be used to generate reliable datasets in a much more efficient manner than experimental approaches. These generated datasets can be input into deep learning models (such as ANN, generative adversarial networks (GAN), etc.) for multiple objective optimization of process conditions and product qualities (Cui et al., 2024; Perré, 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges, significant contributions and future trends: smart technologies, smart cities and digital twins
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed before, foods are susceptible to safety and quality risks at every stage of the supply chain, pre-harvest, but most often post-harvest. Raw material composition and quality, processing, storage and distribution conditions, handling, and even the natural deterioration of foods all contribute to physical, chemical and microbial changes in foods, which make them susceptible to food safety and quality disruptions (Corradini et al., 2024; Singh & Corradini, 2022). As such, to monitor the supply chain effectively, data should be made available and accessible in real-time and integrated at every step (Singh & Corradini, 2022). This is in line with the core principles of smart food traceability, which aims to 1) use sensors and indicators for quick and comprehensive data collection and 2) incorporate digital technologies for cloud computing, learning or the Internet of Things (IoT) (Yu et al., 2022).

Luminescence techniques, including but not limited to collecting single spectra, EEMs, or HSI, allow for rapid, noninvasive, real-time in situ monitoring of pre-harvest and post-harvest processes applied to fresh and minimally processed produce, as seen in the numerous examples provided in this review. However, due to the large amount of data that these methods generate, analysis is often cumbersome, and relevant data, patterns or features are difficult to extract or identify. Using advanced data analytics techniques, including evolutionary machine learning methods, is increasingly gaining popularity and can aid in reducing the data dimensionality for identifying the most useful information to drive and accelerate decision-making processes for food assessment. As such, data analytics applied to luminescence data can potentially improve pre- and post-harvest management (Singh & Corradini, 2022) to report on food quality, safety and identity status along the supply chain more efficiently.
