Additionally, with the improvements in portability (e.g., handheld luminescence spectroscopic devices and smartphones with improved cameras, wireless capabilities, internet connectivity, and processing speed), in-line monitoring followed by rapid, actionable responses is becoming increasingly feasible and practical.

For example, Wang et al. (2024) developed a portable fluorescence spectroscopic system to acquire fluorescence images using a cell phone equipped with a UV-light attachment that were further processed using a purposely developed app. This method was able to detect potential anthrax contamination in foods and the environment using dipicolinic acid (DPA) as a marker. This study also showed potential for real-time analysis of various compounds in foods. Cheng et al. (2018) created a 3D-printed smartphone reader to record spectral images of pesticides in a diverse set of samples. These images were captured using an iPhone 5 equipped with a laser source coupled with a longpass filter and transmission diffraction grating. In this case, smartphone-based spectrometer results have been shown to be comparable to those of commercial spectrophotometers (Yun et al., 2024). Acquiring and processing luminescence data with portable devices for produce monitoring purposes are not only limited to chemical components but also to pathogenic or spoilage microorganisms. Wang, Niu, et al. (2019) developed a smartphone-based fluorescence microscopic system to detect Salmonella spp. that uses video processing techniques to count fluorescent colonies on an app. This indicates the potential applicability of smartphones as an in-line, rapid screening method along the supply chain for post-harvest safety and quality. It is important to note that portable spectrophotometers may lack the precision, sensitivity and resolution of benchtop devices and may be affected to a greater extent by environmental factors (temperature, light, humidity). However, with advances in cameras, portable detectors and internet connectivity, their potential for in-line monitoring should not be discounted. Laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) and light-emitting diode-induced fluorescence (LED-IF), which employ laser radiation and LED as excitation light sources, are often applied to transition fluorescence measurements from the laboratory towards industrial or commercial uses (Ma et al., 2023). LIF and LED-IF have been reported to outperform traditional broadband xenon lamps in terms of cost and sensitivity, making them more suitable for in-line applications (Ma et al., 2023). Further information and discussion on the use of handheld and portable luminescence devices for in-line, commercial and industry, food safety and quality monitoring have been summarized in Ma et al. (2023) and Fu et al. (2024).

There is also an ongoing exploration of state-of-the-art digital tools and techniques, such as the ‘Generative AI’ and ‘Large Language Models’ in the food industry, particularly on how to efficiently use them in food sensing applications in combination with advanced luminescence analytical tools. For example, the Grounded Dino and Segment Anything Model has recently been used together with optical/spectral sensor arrays for plant health status classification (Balasundaram et al., 2024). It is envisaged that more exploration will emerge in this area, which will indefinitely help the global agri-food industry better manage pest and disease stresses, food quality and safety, and the improvement of climate change resilience and food sustainability.

Finally, the use of virtual objects or systems, also known as digital twins, have been gaining traction in the literature for their use in simulating real systems or objects by using complex, real-time data, allowing decision-makers to make more informed and better decisions (Corradini et al., 2024; Udugama et al., 2023; Verboven et al., 2020). Digital twins require the bi-directional flow of data, i.e., the forecasts and predictions made by the digital model inform the actions at the plant or site, which feedback to the model for continuous monitoring and optimization of a system or process (Corradini et al., 2024; Udugama et al., 2023). Improvements in sensor technology, modeling methods and big data analytics are expected to play a role in the development and support of digital twins (Udugama et al., 2023). Digital twins, as they relate to fresh produce, while limited in the literature, have been primarily developed to optimize refrigeration systems and the performance of the produce cold chain (Li et al., 2024). For example, Shoji et al. (2022) used digital twins to monitor the effects of shipment time, storage temperature and humidity on the quality of four different fruits (cucumber, eggplant, raspberry and strawberries) during import to Switzerland from Spain. The authors were able to make recommendations on the optimal shipping/transportation conditions to improve the quality of all fruits based on the performance of the digital twin generated (Shoji et al., 2022). Shrivastava et al. (2022 and 2025) employed digital twins to optimize the hygrothermal conditions of citrus fruits post-harvest. The studies aimed to extend fruit quality, marketability and desirability, based on stakeholder perspectives along the cold supply chain, by examining quality losses in produce due to moisture loss, chilling injuries and risk of condensation. The efficacy of these virtual tools can be further extended by the utilization of additional noninvasive techniques, including luminescence sensing ones, to assess and monitor quality and safety attributes. As such, the use of fluorescence spectroscopic methods, such as EEMs or HIS, although currently underresearched, which already show promise as rapid, non-invasive and low-cost methods with in-line capabilities, can potentially be used for digital twin modeling for continuous supply chain monitoring of pre- and post-harvest processes. Fig. 3 overviews the processes and elements required to develop a digital twin model for pre- and post-harvest monitoring of fresh produce and products using data input from luminescence-based techniques. If used in conjunction with already established big data sources such as quality control measures, environmental monitoring methods, and integrated IoT systems, these interactive digital tools may revolutionize traceability along the supply chain. Therefore, food safety risks, quality breaches, and questionable identity or status of post-harvest foods may not only be flagged for further analysis, but digital twins may also provide solutions to mitigate future occurrences.

However, the use of digital twins and smart technologies for in-line measurements is not straightforward, and many hurdles must be overcome to improve their applicability, particularly in terms of data processing. Firstly, extracting features from the images collected from smartphones that are formatted for downstream machine-learning applications is often difficult (Kalinowska et al., 2021). Secondly, to control for various factors affecting imaging from different cameras and environmental conditions, a large data set is required to construct a data library extensive enough for training these models while minimizing and preventing overfitting (Kalinowska et al., 2021). The curation of these libraries, which may contain thousands or hundreds of thousands of reliably labelled data points, requires extensive resources, procedures and infrastructures and needs to be built in collaboration with large and small-scale producers or stakeholders (Kalinowska et al., 2021). Lastly, developing, maintaining and implementing these systems may be quite costly, and in markets where the profit margins are small, such as the pre- and post-harvest supply chain, small-scale producers may face significant barriers.

The increased need for food traceability systems along the supply chain calls for rapid, non-destructive and cost-effective technologies with in-line capabilities. As such, traditional food traceability methods such as High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR), and DNA sequencing, among others, may be insufficient to monitor the dynamic nature of the food supply chain. Luminescence-based methods, primarily methods that utilize fluorescence-based approaches, have been reviewed for their applicability for pre- and post-harvest process monitoring with applications in food safety assessment, for sorting, grading and authenticity, to better assess the effect of processing on food quality, and to understand metabolic pathways and mechanisms. Of the luminescence-based methods reviewed herein, single point and spectrum acquisition, EEMs, MSI and HSI have in-line capabilities, due to their rapid data acquisition, none or minimal sample preparation requirements and non-invasive and non-destructive nature. Acquiring single point or spectrum measurements is low cost and has simple data processing requirements. However, these approaches are only able to report on a single fluorophore at a time. EEM techniques allow for the acquisition of denser and more complete data sets; however, they are more expensive due to the needed instrumentation, and the recorded data requires more extensive processing. HSI, besides allowing for the simultaneous assessment of multiple fluorophores, offers spatial resolution. MSI and HSI are, however, comparatively more sensitive to environmental interference, light conditions and sensor noise. As such, significant calibration and standardization of data collection methods are essential for reproducibility and consistency of results across equipment and collection sites. Thus, their in-line applications may be limited in the post-harvest supply chain.

Recent advances in technologies and data analysis methods allow for the assessment of multiple features in a sample's fluorescence fingerprint. Due to the amount of data generated by these techniques, multivariate classification and methods capable of recognizing patterns in data are essential for analysis. Machine Learning and AI are increasingly being used in this regard. When coupled with wireless methods such as cloud computing, IoT and smart technology, access to the analytical software or tools is expected to improve. This may enhance their applications for in-line food monitoring and may revolutionize pre- and post-harvest assessment of produce along the supply chain.
